commonMistakes: Memorizing syntax without predicting the underlying value or execution order.
Mutating shared data when the task expects a new value or explicit ownership.
Treating browser APIs and language features as the same layer.
Choosing an abstraction from habit rather than required operations.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

learningObjectives.text: Explain dom tree using a new example and justify the result.
Explain selecting and changing elements using a new example and justify the result.
Explain javascript-browser interaction using a new example and justify the result.

mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **DOM tree** → **Selecting and changing elements** → **JavaScript-browser interaction**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of DOM tree.
choices.label: Identifier lookup starts in the current lexical environment and continues outward until a matching binding is found or the global boundary is reached.

choices.feedback: DOM tree is best understood as follows: The browser parses a document into a tree of node objects. Elements, text, and other nodes have parent-child relationships that scripts can query and change.
choices.label: The browser parses a document into a tree of node objects. Elements, text, and other nodes have parent-child relationships that scripts can query and change.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of DOM tree.
choices.label: The call stack records active execution contexts. Calls push frames and returns pop them. Long synchronous work blocks later JavaScript and delays queued work.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of DOM tree.
choices.label: HTML describes structure, CSS describes presentation, and JavaScript expresses behavior and application logic. It responds to events, changes the document, communicates with servers, and coordinates state.

questions.explanation: DOM tree is best understood as follows: The browser parses a document into a tree of node objects. Elements, text, and other nodes have parent-child relationships that scripts can query and change.
questions.hint: Focus on what the concept changes, enables, or constrains—not only on its syntax.
questions.prompt: Which statement best explains “DOM tree” in the context of The DOM and DOM Manipulation?

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of Selecting and changing elements.
choices.label: Identifier lookup starts in the current lexical environment and continues outward until a matching binding is found or the global boundary is reached.

choices.feedback: Selecting and changing elements is best understood as follows: DOM APIs locate nodes by selector or relationship, then update text, attributes, classes, styles, or structure. Prefer focused updates and class changes over scattered inline mutations.
choices.label: DOM APIs locate nodes by selector or relationship, then update text, attributes, classes, styles, or structure. Prefer focused updates and class changes over scattered inline mutations.

questions.explanation: Selecting and changing elements is best understood as follows: DOM APIs locate nodes by selector or relationship, then update text, attributes, classes, styles, or structure. Prefer focused updates and class changes over scattered inline mutations.

choices.feedback: JavaScript-browser interaction is best understood as follows: Browser APIs provide events, DOM access, networking, storage, and timers. These are host capabilities used from JavaScript, not part of the ECMAScript language itself.

choices.feedback: The reliable sequence starts from the requirement, applies a known rule, observes behavior, and improves the solution afterward.
choices.label: First identify the requirement, then apply the relevant rule from DOM tree, inspect the result, and only then refactor or optimize.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of The DOM and DOM Manipulation.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.
choices.label: Use The DOM and DOM Manipulation when it makes intent, ownership, or data flow clearer; avoid adding it mechanically when a simpler, equally explicit solution is sufficient.

choices.feedback: This does not match the lesson's model. Reconsider the role of The DOM and DOM Manipulation.
choices.label: Use The DOM and DOM Manipulation in every file because more abstraction is always better.

questions.hint: Choose the option that acknowledges both benefits and trade-offs.

sections.body: The browser parses a document into a tree of node objects. Elements, text, and other nodes have parent-child relationships that scripts can query and change.
sections.title: DOM tree

sections.body: DOM APIs locate nodes by selector or relationship, then update text, attributes, classes, styles, or structure. Prefer focused updates and class changes over scattered inline mutations.
sections.title: Selecting and changing elements

sections.body: Browser APIs provide events, DOM access, networking, storage, and timers. These are host capabilities used from JavaScript, not part of the ECMAScript language itself.
sections.title: JavaScript-browser interaction

sections.explanation: DOM APIs select nodes, observe an event, and update text and classes.
sections.title: Worked example

summary: The DOM and DOM Manipulation connects DOM tree, Selecting and changing elements, JavaScript-browser interaction.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: The DOM and DOM Manipulation

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: Learners frequently use the dom and dom manipulation without a clear model of why it behaves as it does; this lesson makes that model explicit. It belongs to the **Browser and DOM Foundations** module.
